questions.q: Who was the Viceroy of India during the Zulu War?
questions.explanation: Lord Lansdowne was the Viceroy of India from 1888 to 1894. The Zulu War took place in 1879.

questions.q: What was the main reason behind the British annexation of Upper Burma in 1885?
opts: (B) To gain control over the Burmese ports
(C) To establish a new trade route
(D) To avenge the death of Warren Hastings
questions.explanation: The British annexed Upper Burma to gain control over the Burmese ports, which were strategically important for trade.

questions.q: How did the Indian National Congress respond to the Ilbert Bill in 1883?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress opposed the Ilbert Bill, which aimed to grant Indian judges the power to try European British subjects.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Minto-Morley Reforms of 1909?
opts: (A) It introduced the principle of dyarchy in the provinces
(B) It granted separate electorates to Muslims
(C) It increased the representation of Indians in the Imperial Legislative Council
(D) It abolished the Council of India
questions.explanation: The Minto-Morley Reforms of 1909 introduced the principle of dyarchy in the provinces, which gave Indians a greater role in provincial administration.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1920?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi led the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1920, which aimed to challenge British rule in India.

questions.q: What was the main objective of the Simon Commission in 1927?
opts: (A) To investigate the working of the Government of India Act, 1935
(B) To propose constitutional reforms for India
(C) To recommend the abolition of the Indian Council of Princes
(D) To prepare a report on the Indian economy
questions.explanation: The Simon Commission was formed to propose constitutional reforms for India in 1935.

questions.q: How did the peasants of Champaran respond to the indigo planters in 1917?
opts: (A) They offered to share the profits
(B) They agreed to pay higher rents
(C) They organized a non-violent resistance movement
questions.explanation: The peasants of Champaran organized a non-violent resistance movement against the indigo planters, which was led by Mahatma Gandhi.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the Quit India Movement in 1942?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi led the Quit India Movement in 1942, which aimed to force the British to leave India.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Rowlatt Act of 1919?
opts: (A) It granted special powers to the Indian courts
(B) It introduced the principle of preventive detention
(C) It abolished the system of trial by jury
(D) It reduced the sentence for crimes committed against the British
questions.explanation: The Rowlatt Act of 1919 introduced the principle of preventive detention, which allowed the British to arrest Indians without trial.

questions.q: Who was the founder of the All India Muslim League in 1906?
questions.explanation: Nawab Salimullah of Dacca founded the All India Muslim League in 1906.

questions.q: What was the main objective of the Khilafat Movement in 1919?
opts: (A) To protect the Ottoman Empire from the Allied Powers
(B) To promote Hindu-Muslim unity in India
(C) To support the Turkish Sultan's claim to the Caliphate
(D) To demand the release of Indian revolutionaries from British prisons
questions.explanation: The Khilafat Movement aimed to support the Turkish Sultan's claim to the Caliphate in 1919.

questions.q: How did the British respond to the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1920?
opts: (B) They suppressed the movement with force
(D) They negotiated with the Indian National Congress
questions.explanation: The British responded to the Non-Cooperation Movement by suppressing it with force in 1920.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the Indian National Army in World War II?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose led the Indian National Army in World War II.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Government of India Act of 1935?
opts: (A) It established a federal structure in India
(B) It introduced the principle of provincial autonomy
(C) It granted full independence to India
(D) It reduced the powers of the Indian provinces
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act of 1935 introduced the principle of provincial autonomy, which gave Indians greater control over provincial administration.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the peasant movement in Telangana in 1946?
questions.explanation: Kondapalli Seetharamaiah led the peasant movement in Telangana in 1946.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Chauri Chaura Satyagraha in 1922?
opts: (A) It marked the beginning of non-violent resistance in India
(B) It highlighted the importance of non-violent resistance in rural India
(C) It led to the arrest of thousands of Indians
(D) It resulted in the death of over 200 British soldiers
questions.explanation: The Chauri Chaura Satyagraha led to the arrest of thousands of Indians in 1922.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the peasants in the Telangana region during the Telangana Armed Struggle?
questions.explanation: Kondapalli Seetharamaiah led the peasants in the Telangana region during the Telangana Armed Struggle.

questions.q: What was the main objective of the Tebhaga Movement in Bengal in 1946?
opts: (A) To reduce the rent for sharecroppers
(B) To increase the share of peasants in the crop
(C) To demand higher wages for agricultural laborers
(D) To promote communal harmony in Bengal
questions.explanation: The Tebhaga Movement aimed to increase the share of peasants in the crop in Bengal in 1946.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the peasants in the Bihar Peasant Movement in 1946?
questions.explanation: Swami Sahajanand Saraswati led the peasants in the Bihar Peasant Movement in 1946.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Andhra Mahasabha in 1919?
opts: (A) It was a regional organization that promoted Telugu language and culture
(B) It was a movement that demanded the separation of Andhra from Madras Presidency
(C) It was a peasant movement that demanded land reforms
(D) It was a nationalist movement that demanded Indian independence
questions.explanation: The Andhra Mahasabha was a regional organization that promoted Telugu language and culture in 1919.

questions.q: How did the British respond to the Andhra Mahasabha in 1919?
opts: (B) They suppressed the movement with force
(D) They negotiated with the Andhra Mahasabha leaders
questions.explanation: The British responded to the Andhra Mahasabha by ignoring the movement in 1919.

questions.q: Who was the leader of the peasant movement in the Deccan region in 1918?
questions.explanation: B.R. Ambedkar led the peasant movement in the Deccan region in 1918.
